en.extract:
The heart-spotted woodpecker (Hemicircus canente) is a species of bird in the woodpecker family. It has a contrasting black and white plumage, a distinctively stubby body and a large wedge-shaped head making it easy to identify while its frequent calling make it easy to detect as it forage for invertebrates under the bark of the slender outer branches of trees. They move about in pairs or small groups and are often found in mixed-species foraging flocks. They have a wide distribution across Asia with populations in the forests of southwestern and central India which are slightly separated from their ranges in the Himalayas and Southeast Asia.


Taxonomy
The heart-spotted woodpecker was described in 1832 by the French naturalist René Lesson from a specimen collected from Bago in Myanmar. He coined the binomial name Picus canente. The specific name is from Roman mythology: Canens was a sweet-voiced nymph, her husband was Picus. The species is now placed in the genus Hemicircus  that was introduced in 1837 by the English naturalist William Swainson. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description

A small, uniquely shaped, black and buff woodpecker with a large crest making the head look large for the short body and tail. Both males and females are predominantly black with heart-shaped black spots on white shoulders with broad white scapular patches and barring of flight feathers. The female has the forehead and crown buffy white while on males it is black with small white dots. The throat is whitish and the underparts are dark olive grey. A tuft of feathers on the back are specialized and are lipid rich which causes the feathers to stick together in preserved specimens. These special feathers or "fat quills" sometimes make the rump feathers appear buff and may be a form of "cosmetic colouration" and the secretion is said to have a pleasant smell but the functional significance is unknown.


Habitat and distribution
Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests. They are found in the Himalayan forests of India, and extend into Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia and Vietnam. Within India, they are also found in the Western Ghats and the forests of central India. A subspecies cordatus based on the description by Thomas C. Jerdon from a specimen from the Western Ghats is not considered distinct and the populations differ slightly in plumage colour and vary clinally in size (the northern birds being larger than those closer to the equator).


Behaviour and ecology

These woodpeckers forage in pairs, sometimes joining mixed-species foraging flocks. They fly from tree to tree in a sharply bounding flight giving a head-heavy appearance. They forage on thin branches and call often. They feed mainly on insects under bark but have been known to peck the pods of Cassia fistula to obtain insect larvae. The calls include a sharp twee-twee-twee (duet call) sometimes leading to a trill of several notes, a nasal ki-yeew and repeated su-sie calls. They drum infrequently during the breeding season, mainly during winter and before the Monsoons. The nest is bored in a dead branch with a narrow 3 to 4 cm diameter opening and the tunnel going down obliquely about 20 cm before widening into a chamber. Nests may sometimes be built in fence posts. The usual clutch is two or three eggs, which are unmarked white. Ticks of the species Haemaphysalis spinigera have been noted on these birds.


References


Other sources
Menon, G.K. (1985) On the source of 'resin' in the plumage of heart spotted woodpecker, Hemicircus canente. Pavo 23(1&2), 107–109.
Neelakantan, K.K. (1965) The nesting of the Heartspotted Woodpecker. Newsletter for Birdwatchers .   5(3), 6–8.


External links
Photographs, calls and videos

The '''heart-spotted woodpecker''' ('''''Hemicircus canente''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the [[Picidae|woodpecker]] family. It has a contrasting black and white plumage, a distinctively stubby body and a large wedge-shaped head making it easy to identify while its frequent calling make it easy to detect as it forage for invertebrates under the bark of the slender outer branches of trees. They move about in pairs or small groups and are often found in [[mixed-species foraging flock]]s. They have a wide distribution across Asia with populations in the forests of southwestern and central India which are slightly separated from their ranges in the [[Himalayas]] and Southeast Asia.

==Taxonomy==
The heart-spotted woodpecker was [[species description|described]] in 1832 by the French naturalist [[René Lesson]] from a specimen collected from [[Bago, Myanmar|Bago]] in [[Myanmar]]. He coined the [[binomial name]] ''Picus canente''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Lesson | first=René P. | author-link=René Lesson | year=1830 | title=Centurie zoologique, ou, Choix d'animaux rares, nouveaux ou imparfaitement connus: enrichi de planches inédites, dessinées d'après nature par M. Prêtre, gravées et coloriées avec le plus grand soin | location=Paris | publisher=Chez F.G. Levrault | page=215 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/28724814 }} Although the title page gives the year as 1830, the book was not published until 1832.</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1948 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=6 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=223 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14477656 }}</ref> The specific name is from Roman mythology: [[Canens (mythology)|Canens]] was a sweet-voiced nymph, her husband was [[Picus]].<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. |author-link=James A. Jobling | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url=https://archive.org/details/helmdictionarysc00jobl_997 | url-access=limited | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=[https://archive.org/details/helmdictionarysc00jobl_997/page/n88 88] }}</ref> The species is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Hemicircus ]]'' that was introduced in 1837 by the English naturalist [[William Swainson]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Swainson | first=William | author-link=William Swainson | year=1837 | title=On the Natural History and Classification of Birds | volume=2 | place=London  | publisher=John Taylor | page=306 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41945084 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | year=2020 | title=Woodpeckers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/woodpeckers/ | publisher=[[International Ornithologists' Union]] | access-date=15 May 2020 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>

==Description==
[[File:Heart spotted woodpecker dandeli.jpg|thumb|left|Female heart spotted woodpecker on a fig tree at Dandeli tiger reserve, India]]
A small, uniquely shaped, black and buff woodpecker with a large [[Crest (feathers)|crest]] making the head look large for the short body and tail. Both males and females are predominantly black with heart-shaped black spots on white shoulders with broad white scapular patches and barring of [[flight feathers]]. The female has the forehead and crown buffy white while on males it is black with small white dots. The throat is whitish and the underparts are dark olive grey. A tuft of feathers on the back are specialized and are lipid rich which causes the feathers to stick together in preserved specimens.<ref>{{cite journal|volume=113|issue=2|journal=Ibis|pages =234–238|year=1971|title='Resin secretion' in ''Hemicircus'' (Picidae)|author1=Bock, WJ  |author2=LL Short Jr|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1971.tb05149.x}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Menon GK |author2=J Menon |title=Avian epidermal lipids: functional considerations and relationship to feathering |journal=American Zoologist |year=2000|volume=40|issue=4|pages=540–552 |doi=10.1093/icb/40.4.540 |doi-access=free }}</ref> These special feathers or "fat quills" sometimes make the rump feathers appear buff and may be a form of "cosmetic colouration" and the secretion is said to have a pleasant smell but the functional significance is unknown.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1086/510095|journal=American Naturalist|year=2007|title=Cosmetic coloration in birds: occurrence, function, and evolution | author1=Delhey, K | author2=A Peters | author3=B Kempenaers | volume=169 | pages=S145–S158 | url=http://mail.orn.mpg.de/documents/kempenaers/Kemp_66.pdf|pmid=19426089|s2cid=29592388|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110719062924/http://mail.orn.mpg.de/documents/kempenaers/Kemp_66.pdf|archive-date=2011-07-19}}</ref><ref name=hbk/><ref>{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/faunaofbritishin025218mbp#page/n81/mode/1up/|pages=68–70|title=The Fauna of British India, Including Ceylon and Burma. Birds. Volume 3|year=1895|author=Blanford WT|publisher=Taylor and Francis, London}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Hargitt, Edward |author-link=Edward Hargitt |year=1884 |title=Notes on woodpeckers VIII. The genus ''Hemicircus''| journal=Ibis| volume=26|issue=3|pages=244–259|url=https://archive.org/stream/ibis25brit#page/244/mode/1up/|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1884.tb01163.x}}</ref>

==Habitat and distribution==
Its natural [[habitat]] is [[subtropical]] or [[tropical]] moist lowland [[forest]]s. They are found in the Himalayan forests of India, and extend into Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia and Vietnam. Within India, they are also found in the Western Ghats and the forests of central India.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Kanoje, R |year=1995| title=Heartspotted Woodpecker in the Kanha National Park| journal= Newsletter for Birdwatchers |volume=35|issue=5|page=96|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW35_5#page/n17/mode/1up/}}</ref> A subspecies ''cordatus'' based on the description by [[Thomas C. Jerdon]] from a specimen from the [[Western Ghats]] is not considered distinct and the populations differ slightly in plumage colour and vary [[cline (biology)|clinally]] in size (the northern birds being larger than those closer to the equator).<ref name=hbk/><ref>{{cite journal|author=Ali,S |year=1951|title=The Heart-spotted Woodpecker - ''Hemicircus canente''|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=49| issue=4|pages=786–787|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48182350}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=Jerdon,T C |author-link=Thomas C. Jerdon |year=1840 |title=Catalogue of the birds of the peninsula of India |journal=Madras Journal of Literature and Science |volume=11 |issue=27 |pages=209–239 |url=https://archive.org/stream/madrasjournalli00browgoog#page/n227/mode/1up/}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
[[File:Heart Spotted Woodpecker(Female).jpg|thumb|upright|right|Female]]
These woodpeckers forage in pairs, sometimes joining [[mixed-species foraging flock]]s. They fly from tree to tree in a sharply bounding flight giving a head-heavy appearance. They forage on thin branches and call often. They feed mainly on insects under bark but have been known to peck the pods of ''[[Cassia fistula]]'' to obtain insect larvae.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Santharam | first=V. |year=1998| title=Woodpeckers feeding on ''Cassia'' pods| journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society | volume=95|issue=3| pages=505–506| url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48605055}}</ref> The calls include a sharp ''twee-twee-twee'' (duet call) sometimes leading to a trill of several notes,<ref>{{cite journal|author=Betts, FN|author-link=Frederick Nicholson Betts|year=1934|title=South Indian woodpeckers|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=37|pages=197–203|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48185473}}</ref> a nasal ''ki-yeew''<ref name=pcr>{{cite book|author1=Rasmussen PC |author1-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen |author2=JC Anderton |year=2005|title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide | volume=2|publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions|page=291}}</ref> and repeated ''su-sie'' calls.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Duetting calls of the Heart-spotted Woodpeckers ''Hemicircus canente'' (Lesson)|last=Santharam | first=V.|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |year=2004| volume=101| issue=1|pages=157–158|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48568077}}</ref> They drum infrequently during the breeding season, mainly during winter and before the Monsoons. The nest is bored in a dead branch with a narrow 3 to 4 cm diameter opening and the tunnel going down obliquely about 20 cm before widening into a chamber. Nests may sometimes be built in fence posts.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Inglis |first=Charles M |author-link=Charles M. Inglis |year=1931|title=The nesting of the Malabar Heart-spotted Woodpecker (''Hemicircus canante cordatus'') in Travancore|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=35|issue=1|pages=207–208|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47540177}}</ref> The usual clutch is two or three eggs, which are unmarked white.<ref name=hbk>{{cite book|last1=Ali | first1=S.  |last2=Ripley | first2=S.D.| pages=236–237|title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan | volume=4|edition=2nd|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=1983}}</ref> Ticks of the species ''[[Haemaphysalis spinigera]]'' have been noted on these birds.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Rajagopalan, PK |year=1972| title=Ixodid ticks (Acarina: Ixodidae) parasitizing wild birds in the Kyasanur forest disease area of Shimoga District, Mysore State, India|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=69|issue=1|pages=55–78|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48288893}}</ref>

zh.extract:
黑冠啄木鸟（學名：Hemicircus canente）一种分布于南亚和东南亚中南半岛等地区的䴕形目鸟类，隶属于啄木鸟科黑冠啄木鸟属。本种由法国鸟类学家勒内·普里姆韦尔·莱松于1830年描述发表。


参考文献


延伸阅读
